coredata.dc:title: One-step ratiometric fluorescence sensing of ascorbic acid in food samples by carbon dots-referenced lanthanide probe
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry
coredata.dc:creator: Tian, Xiaolin
Fan, Zhefeng
coredata.dc:description: We report a ratiometric fluorescence sensor formed via the self-assembly of the lanthanide ion Eu3+ and blue-emitting carbon dots (CDs) for the direct detection of ascorbic acid (AA). The obtained sensor exhibited the characteristic emission of 2,6-pyridinedicarboxylic acid (DPA)-sensitized Eu3+ (Eu–DPA) at 615 nm and the weak emission of CDs at 440 nm under excitation at 283 nm. After the addition of AA, the fluorescence emission of Eu–DPA, which served as the AA recognition site, was significantly quenched, whereas the emission of CDs changed slightly, resulting in a ratiometric fluorescent response toward AA. A wide linear range from 0.5 μM to 820 μM was achieved in the detection of AA with an LOD of 60 nM. Moreover, this CDs/Eu–DPA sensor, which displayed excellent sensitivity and selectivity, was successfully applied for the determination of AA in fruits, vegetables and beverage samples, suggesting a great potential for applications in foods.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Food analysis
Lanthanide probe
Carbon dots
Ascorbic acid
Ratiometric fluorescence sensor

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Determination of AA in real food samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image of CDs (Inset: size distribution histogram of CDs). (b) HRTEM image of CDs.
(a) FTIR spectra of CDs, Eu–DPA, and CDs/Eu–DPA. (b) UV–vis spectra of CDs, Eu–DPA, CDs/Eu–DPA, AA and CDs/Eu–DPA+AA.
(a) Full-spectrum XPS image of CDs/Eu–DPA. (b) C1s spectrum of CDs/Eu–DPA. (c) N1s spectrum of CDs/Eu–DPA. (d) O1s spectrum of CDs/Eu–DPA.
(a) Effects of pH on the CDs/Eu–DPA fluorescence system and experimental results. (b) Effects of time on experimental results.

(a) Emission spectra of CDs/Eu–DPA in the presence of AA with different concentrations (Inset: photograph of CDs/Eu–DPA under irradiation with UV light when AA concentration is varied [0, 200, and 500 μM from left to the right]). (b) Linear relationship between fluorescence intensity ratio (F615/F440) of CDs/Eu–DPA and the concentrations of AA.
(a) Selectivity and anti-interference of AA detection. (b) Effects of AA, d-Isoascorbic acid (D-Iso AA), and dehydroascorbic acid (Dehydro AA) on the CDs/Eu–DPA fluorescence system.
Schematic illustration of the CDs/Eu–DPA sensor for detection of AA.
Analysis results and recovery rate of AA from food samples.

head.ce:title: One-step ratiometric fluorescence sensing of ascorbic acid in food samples by carbon dots-referenced lanthanide probe

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Material Science, Shanxi Normal University, Linfen, 041004, People’s Republic of China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Material Science

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Material Science, Shanxi Normal University, Linfen, 041004, People’s Republic of China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: We report a ratiometric fluorescence sensor formed via the self-assembly of the lanthanide ion Eu3+ and blue-emitting carbon dots (CDs) for the direct detection of ascorbic acid (AA). The obtained sensor exhibited the characteristic emission of 2,6-pyridinedicarboxylic acid (DPA)-sensitized Eu3+ (Eu–DPA) at 615 nm and the weak emission of CDs at 440 nm under excitation at 283 nm. After the addition of AA, the fluorescence emission of Eu–DPA, which served as the AA recognition site, was significantly quenched, whereas the emission of CDs changed slightly, resulting in a ratiometric fluorescent response toward AA. A wide linear range from 0.5 μM to 820 μM was achieved in the detection of AA with an LOD of 60 nM. Moreover, this CDs/Eu–DPA sensor, which displayed excellent sensitivity and selectivity, was successfully applied for the determination of AA in fruits, vegetables and beverage samples, suggesting a great potential for applications in foods.

ce:section.ce:para: Lanthanide ions exhibit extraordinary spectroscopic features, such as large Stokes and/or anti-Stokes displacement, long luminescence lifetimes, and narrow emission bands, making them widely employed in the analysis field [1,2]. However, lanthanides are difficult to excite directly due to their weak f–f transition absorption, which originates from their low absorption cross-sections. Indirect excitation by a sensitive chromophore or antenna has been proven to be an effective way to solve this problem [3,4]. 2,6-Pyridinedicarboxylic acid (DPA) is the sole component of dormant bacterial spores. Given its chemical structure, DPA can not only form coordinate bonds with lanthanide ions through carboxyl and N atoms but also sensitize the fluorescence of lanthanide ions through energy adsorption by benzene rings. These characteristics suggest that DPA is an ideal fluorescence sensitizer of lanthanide ions [5].

Carbon dots (CDs), an emerging fluorescence material, have attracted tremendous attention for the characteristics of high fluorescence intensity, good photostability, hypotoxicity, high water solubility, and remarkable biocompatibility. With continuous improvements in preparation techniques and surface functionalization, the original performance of CDs has been optimized considerably. Thus, CDs provide a promising alternative to fluorescent dyes and metal quantum dots in the analytical field [6–8]. Nevertheless, CDs-based sensors generally suffer strong interferences from the surrounding microenvironment during the detection of targets in complex matrixes [2]. Ratiometric fluorescence sensors are accepted as an ideal substitute because they can utilize the ratio of double radiation strengths as the output signal under single excitation. Under ideal conditions, ratiometric fluorescence sensors are similar to internal calibrators when used in analysis. This similarity reflects an increased signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) and suitability for the highly sensitive and selective detection of targets in complex matrixes [9]. In addition, lanthanide ions with strong coordination capability and CDs that contain rich surface groups can form a new ratiometric fluorescence probe through self-assembly, thus reducing the risk of false-positives during detection and improving detection sensitivity considerably.

Ascorbic acid (AA), also known as vitamin C, is an essential vitamin for maintaining healthy physiological functions [10]. However, AA cannot be synthesized in the human body and can only be acquired from the external environment through the ingestion of food and drugs [11]. The occurrence and development of many diseases, such as cancer, cardiovascular diseases, and Parkinson’s disease, are related to AA deficiency [12]. In food industry, AA is also used as an antioxidant [13]. Therefore, the detection of AA is crucial to maintaining the physical health of the human body and the development of the food industry. Numerous methods, such as high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [14–16], 2,6-dichloroindophenol titration [17], ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) spectrophotometry [18,19], electrochemical processes [20–22], and fluorescence methods including in vivo bio-imaging studies [23–27], for AA detection exist. The fluorescence method is highly appreciated for its characteristics of high sensitivity, wide linear range, and good selectivity. Lanthanide ions and CDs possess promising application prospects as abundant fluorescence probes in the detection of AA. Most existing fluorescence probes based on lanthanide ions require the preparation of complex coordinated polymers [28–30]. Moreover, most AA sensors based on CDs combine indirectly via the redox strategy between metal ions and AA [31–35]. AA fluorescence probes for direct detection have important research importance because they can simplify operation and decrease impurity interference.

Herein, a ratiometric fluorescence sensor was developed for the direct detection of AA by using the CDs/Eu–DPA system, which was fabricated through the self-assembly of DPA-sensitized Eu3+ and blue-emitting CDs (Scheme 1 ). Blue-emitting CDs were synthesized through the one-pot hydrothermal method by applying citric acid and urea as precursors. Given the presence of abundant O-containing functional groups, CDs can directly coordinate with Eu3+ in Eu–DPA to form the CDs /Eu–DPA sensor at room temperature. AA could quench the fluorescence of Eu3+ significantly with negligible influences on CDs. Therefore, the CDs/Eu–DPA ratiometric fluorescence sensor is applicable for the quantitative detection of AA in foods.

Europium nitrate hexahydrate (Eu(NO₃)₃·6H₂O) and 2,6-pyridinedicarboxylic acid (DPA) were bought from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Citric acid (CA), urea, acetic acid, sodium acetate, ascorbic acid (AA), dextrose (Dex), KI, NaCl, CaCl2, MgCl2, fructose (Fru), threonine (Thr), arginine (Arg), alanine (Ala), l-Phenylalanine (L-Phe), l-cysteine (L-cys), and glutathione (GSH) were purchased from Tianjin Guangfu Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd (Tianjin, China). d-Isoascorbic acid (D-Iso AA) and dehydroascorbic acid (Dehydro AA) were procured from Shanghai Yuanye Technological Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). The dialysis bag (MW: 1000) was provided by Union Carbide Co. (USA). Acetic acid–sodium acetate buffer (0.2 M) was prepared with acetic acid and sodium acetate. In this experiment, only analytically pure reagents were used, and ultrapure water was collected from a Milli-Q purification system (USA).

The morphologies of CDs were observed under a Tecnai G 2 F20 transmission electron microscope (TEM, FEI Instrument Co., Ltd, USA). Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were recorded on a Niocolet 380 spectrograph (Var-ian Instrument Co., Ltd, USA). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) were performed with Thermo EscalAB 250Xi (Thermoelectric Corporation, USA). UV–vis adsorption spectra were collected by using a Cary 300 UV/Visible spectrophotometer (Var-ian Instrument Co., Ltd, USA). Fluorescence spectra were obtained by using a LS-55 fluorescence/phosphor spectrophotometer (Perkin-Elmer Company, USA). Fluorescence lifetime were measured by applying a JY-Horiba Fluorolog-3 fluorescence spectrophotometer.

The synthesis of CDs was optimized in accordance with a reported method [36]. Citric acid (0.1 g) and urea (0.7 g) were dissolved in 30 mL of ultrapure water, and the solution was transferred to a stainless steel autoclave with a Teflon liner after complete dissolution. The solution was then subjected to thermal treatment for 4 h at 180 ℃. After natural cooling, the solution was filtered with a piece of 0.22 μm drainage filtering membrane. The filtrate was purified through dialysis (MW: 1000) until no fluorescence could be detected outside the dialysis bag. Finally, the liquid in the dialysis bag was frozen and dried to obtain a light brown powder.

The preparation of CDs/Eu–DPA was appropriately optimized in accordance with the reported method [9]. First, 10 mL of DPA aqueous solution (10 mM) and 1 mL of 100 mM Eu(NO₃)₃·6H₂O were mixed through magnetic stirring for 0.5 h. Subsequently, 5 mL of CDs (1 mg/mL) was added and stirred at room temperature overnight. Finally, the mixed solution was processed in a dialysis bag for 48 h (MW: 1000) to eliminate excessive Eu, DPA, and Eu–DPA. The solution in the dialysis bag was brought to a volume of 20 mL with water. Then, the mixture was preserved for subsequent characterization and experiments. Given that most CDs could not pass through the dialysis bag (MW: 1000) after the previous purification, the concentration of CDs (0.4 mg/mL) was determined as the concentration of CDs/Eu–DPA.

First, 100 μL of pH 6 acetic acid–sodium acetate buffer solution (0.2 M) and 200 μL of CDs/Eu–DPA (0.4 mg/mL) solution were added successively into a 10 mL colorimetric tube. Next, a series of AA solutions with different concentrations was added. Water was added again until the volume of 2.5 mL was reached. The mixture was stabilized at room temperature for 5 min. Fluorescence intensities at the emission wavelengths of 440 and 615 nm were recorded under 283 nm excitation. Signal output values were calculated on the basis of experimental data by using F615/F440.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of AA in real food samples
ce:section.ce:para: Four different food samples (orange juice, compound juice beverage, kiwi and yellow bell pepper) with different AA levels were chosen to evaluate the effect of the proposed method. Detection procedures of AA in these food samples were the same as in the standard samples. Besides, the standard addition method was used for analysis of AA in the food samples to evaluate the feasibility and accuracy of fluorescent sensing with the CDs/Eu–DPA sensor for real applications. The extracting method of AA from each food sample was partly according to the literature [35]. Food samples (orange juice and compound juice beverage) were centrifuged for 10 min at 7000 rpm. The supernatant was filtered by using a 0.22 μm organic membrane, diluted to the appropriate concentrations, and finally stored for subsequent experiments. The edible part of food samples (kiwi fruit and yellow bell pepper) were collected for juice preparation. The collected stock solution was centrifuged for 10 min under 7000 rpm. The supernatant was filtered by using a 0.22 μm organic membrane, diluted to appropriate concentrations, and finally stored for subsequent experiment. All food samples were bought from supermarkets in Linfen City, China.

The size and morphology of CDs were characterized by TEM. As seen in Fig. 1 a, CDs are uniformly distributed and well-dispersed. The particle size of CDs was 2–10 nm with an average diameter of 5 nm. The high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image (Fig. 1b) shows that the CDs have obvious lattice fringes, indicating that the as-prepared CDs possess a graphite-like structure.

To further verify the connection between CDs and Eu–DPA, Fourier transfer infrared (FTIR) spectra of CDs, Eu–DPA, and CDs/Eu–DPA were investigated. As shown in Fig. 2 a, compared with the FTIR spectrum of CDs, that of CDs/Eu–DPA has a blue shift or red shift of the adsorption peak at OH (from 3194 to 3180 cm-1), COOH(from 1707 to 1724 cm−1), and NH (from 771 to 761 cm−1). The existence and stretching vibrations of these groups might facilitate the coordination of N-CDs with Eu-DPA [37].

The UV–vis spectra of CDs, Eu–DPA and CDs/Eu–DPA were provided in Fig. 2b. The weak shoulder peak of CDs at 242 nm is attributed to the formation of multiple aromatic chromophores that may have originated from π–π* transition. In addition, the strong absorption peak at 329 nm may be a consequence of n–π* transition corresponding to the carbonyl/amine functional groups [38]. Eu–DPA has strong adsorption peaks at 271 and 279 nm, which are the characteristic absorption peaks of coordination between trivalent lanthanide ions and DPA [39,40] and is consistent with the variation of the absorption peak due to π–π* transition between trivalent lanthanide ions and aromatic ligands [41]. The strong adsorption peak of CDs/Eu–DPA presents a blue shift of 2 nm in contrast to that of CDs, indicating that the binding between CDs and Eu–DPA is not a simple physical adsorption.

The surface composition and elements of CDs/Eu–DPA were analyzed through XPS (Fig. 3 ). Full-spectrum XPS analysis indicates that CDs/Eu–DPA is composed of C, N, O, and Eu, which exhibit peaks at 285, 400, 533, and 1073 eV, respectively. The high-resolution C1s spectrum has three peaks at 284.8, 285.8, and 288.6 eV that correspond to C–C (sp3), C–N (sp3), and O–C=O (sp2), respectively. The high-resolution N1s and O1s spectra further prove the existence of C–N (399.8 eV), N–O (402.1 eV), C–O (531.9 eV), and C=O (533.2 eV) bonds in mixed CDs. This result further proves that CDs/Eu–DPA contains abundant surface groups, such as −COOH, −OH, and −NH2, providing potential sites for sensing.

The possible sensing mechanism of the ratiometric nanosensor is introduced in Scheme 1. First, amino and carboxyl groups in the aromatic ring of DPA can form strong coordination with Eu3+ to replace H2O in the center of Eu3+ completely and strengthen the fluorescence of Eu3+, thus forming Eu–DPA [39]. The N-doped CDs, which were synthesized with citric acid and urea through the hydrothermal method, contain numerous functional groups and can conjugate with Eu3+ through surface amino group and carboxyl [9]. Moreover, the fluorescence intensity of CDs is negatively correlated with Eu3+ concentration. On this basis, Eu–DPA and CDs trigger self-assembly to form the CDs/Eu–DPA nanosensor.

The probe has two emission peaks attributable to CDs and Eu–DPA. After adding AA, it mainly quenched the fluorescence of Eu–DPA, but it also affected the fluorescence of CDs. In order to explore the quenching mechanism, related experiments were carried out. (1) As can be seen from Fig. 2b, the main peak positions of the fluorescence system had not changed obviously after adding AA, which means that AA had not completely replaced DPA or produced a ternary complex with the Eu–DPA. (2) In addition, the UV absorption range of AA is about 230∼300 nm, showing partial coincidence with the excitation peak (283 nm) of the experiment (Fig. S1). It is suggested that the quenching of Eu3+ at 615 nm may be partly due to the internal filtering effect (IFE), which also explains the change of CDs fluorescence at 440 nm after adding AA [42]. (3) Besides, as can be seen from Fig. S2, after AA addition, the fluorescence lifetime of the CDs/Eu–DPA fluorescence system at 440 nm has no obvious change, and that at 615 nm decreased significantly from its original 683.63 to 510.60 μs.It is worth noting that the fluorescence of Eu3+ in the probe was derived from the energy transfer of the ligand DPA to Eu3+. Therefore, in addition to the IFE, the decrease in the fluorescence of europium should be related to the interference with the energy transfer of DPA to Eu3+. Coupled with the change in fluorescence lifetime, the most likely reason was that AA affected the coordination microenvironment of DPA and Eu3+, or the interaction between DPA and AA existed, causing Europium to be quenched through the non-radiative inactivation process [40].

We also note that the added AA has some effect on the CDs fluorescence due to the IFE, which is not an ideal phenomenon in the ratio fluorescence probe. But interestingly, as can be seen from Fig. S3, when the CDs fluorescence is taken as the reference signal, the output signal changes from F615 to F615/F440, presenting a wider linearity than the Eu–DPA system. This indicates that the CDs/Eu-DPA fluorescence system may partly eliminate the background effect of IFE through internal calibrating, showing the real advantage of ratiometric fluorescence sensor.
In order to obtain the best response signal, the main experimental factors including reaction time and pH of reaction solution were optimized.

The effects of AA on the fluorescence quenching of CDs/Eu–DPA when pH ranges from 3 to 9 were tested (Fig. 4 a). System fluorescence and quenching efficiency are stable when the pH is between 6–9. Given that AA is easily degraded in neutral and alkali environments, the pH was set at 6 in the subsequent sample detection experiments. Moreover, the fluorescence and quenching efficiency of CDs/Eu–DPA are relatively low in strongly acidic environments likely because of the protonation of surface carboxyls and the reduced activity of amino groups in the system [9].

In addition, the effects of reaction time on the quenching efficiency of the system were investigated. The results are shown in Fig. 4b. Fluorescence intensity stabilizes at 5 min after AA addition. However, the florescence intensity remains constant for 1 h, indicating that the reaction between AA and florescence system is rapid and stable. As a result, the incubation time for the follow-up experiment was determined as 5 min.

Under the optimal experimental conditions, the sensing performance of the CDs/Eu–DPA system for AA detection was studied. As shown in Fig. 5 a, with the increasing AA concentration, the fluorescence intensity located at 615 nm decreased gradually, By contrast, the degree of fluorescence quenching at 440 nm is relatively low. By Plotting (F615/F440) versus the values of AA concentration (Fig. 5b), the linear response range from 0.5 to 820 μM can be derived, and the detection limit (LOD) calculated to be 60 nM (3σ). Compared with reported fluorescent sensors for AA detection (Table S1), the existing CDs/Eu–DPA fluorescence system has a wide linear range and a low limit of detection, possessing promising application prospects in food analysis and detection.

With the aim of evaluating the selectivity and anti-interference performance, the influences of nine possible coexisting substances (Na+, K+, Mg2+, Ca2+, I−, Cl−, dextrose, fructose, citric acid, threonine, arginine, alanine, l-cysteine, glutathione, and l-phenylalanine) on the CDs/Eu–DPA fluorescence system were analyzed (Fig. 6 a). Under the same detection conditions, the fluorescence system shows no significant changes even when the concentration of the nine substances is twice that of AA (500 μM).

The anti-interference experiment shows that the fluorescence of the system remains basically constant when AA (500 μM) is present along with nine substances at 2-fold concentrations. Therefore, the sensor detection of AA is not influenced by the coexistence of the above nine substances and AA.

In addition, the influences of AA and its two analogues (D-Isoascorbic acid and dehydroascorbic acid) on the fluorescence system were compared. As seen from Fig. 6b, d-Isoascorbic has considerable quenching efficiency for the CDs/Eu–DPA fluorescence system. By contrast, dehydroascorbic acid negligibly affects the CDs/Eu–DPA fluorescence system. It is well known that ascorbic acid is easily oxidized to dehydroascorbic acid, thus the sensor can detect the available content of ascorbic acid. d-Isoascorbic acid, an optical isomer of AA, has similar chemical properties and physiological activity as AA and is mainly applied in the antioxidant preservation of foods. Therefore, in the general assays for AA, no distinction is made between the two substances.
To summarize, the proposed sensor presents good selectivity and anti-interference in AA detection.

The applicability and reliability of the proposed CDs/Eu–DPA fluorescence system were assessed by using two different fresh fruit and vegetable(kiwi and yellow bell pepper) and two different commodity beverages (orange juice and compound juice beverage) to investigate the performances of the CDs/Eu–DPA fluorescence system in practical AA detection. The results are listed in Table 1 . The proposed CDs/Eu–DPA fluorescence system shows that the AA contents of fresh kiwi fruit are 34.67 mg/100 g and those of bell pepper are 57.24 mg/100 g. In addition, the AA contents of industrial food products (orange juice and compound juice beverage) per 100 g are 10.90 and 16.16 mg. Moreover, the accuracy of the proposed CDs/Eu–DPA fluorescence system was assessed via a labeling recovery experiment. As shown in Table 1, the average labeling recovery rate after three repeated experiments is 92.73 %–103.57 % (RSD ≤ 5.81 %), indicating that the CDs/Eu–DPA fluorescence system is a flexible, rapid, accurate, and reliable analytical method.

Blue-emitting CDs were used as the objects and self-assembled into a fluorescence analysis system based on the rare-earth ion Eu3+. A ratiometric fluorescence sensor was constructed by using the blue-light-emitting CDs as a reference signal and DPA-sensitized Eu3+ as the response signal under single wavelength excitation. This system was applied to detect AA in foods. Blue-emitting CDs were synthesized with citric acid and urea through the one-step hydrothermal method. The abundant O-containing functional groups present on the surfaces of CDs can coordinate with Eu3+ in Eu–DPA at room temperature directly to form the CDs/Eu–DPA sensor. AA can decrease the fluorescence of Eu3+ significantly without exerting substantial effects on CDs. Hence, the CDs/Eu–DPA system can enable the quantitative detection of AA in foods. The results reveal a good linear relationship between the concentration of AA (0.5–820 μM) and the fluorescence response signal. The lowest limit of detection is 60 nM. Moreover, the CDs/Eu–DPA sensor has been applied successfully to detect AA in several fresh fruits, fresh vegetables, and commodity beverages. It showed an average labeling recovery rate of 92.73 %–103.57 % (RSD ≤ 5.81 %). In contrast to previous methods, the proposed CDs/Eu–DPA sensor could detect AA directly rather than depending on the redox strategy of metal ions. Given its simple operation, low limit of detection, wide linear range, and good selectivity, the proposed sensor has considerable application potential in food analysis and detection.
This article does not contain any studies with human participants or animals performed by any of the authors.
Informed consent was obtained from all individual participants included in the study.
Xiaolin Tian: Investigation, Writing - original draft, Formal analysis, Writing - Review & Editing. Zhefeng Fan: Writing - Review & Editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jphotochem.2021.113261.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Recent developments in lanthanide-based luminescent probes
Coord. Chem. Rev.

Coord. Chem. Rev.

Acc. Chem. Res.

Angew. Chem. Int. Ed. Engl.
